segments.enText: [Placeholder] Barchu is the public call to prayer. The chazan invites and the congregation responds — tefillah is never just individual, it's communal.

segments.heText: שְׁמַע יִשְׂרָאֵל יְיָ אֱלֹהֵינוּ יְיָ אֶחָד.
בָּרוּךְ שֵׁם כְּבוֹד מַלְכוּתוֹ לְעוֹלָם וָעֶד.

segments.enText: [Placeholder] The Shema is not a prayer — it's a declaration. We aren't asking for anything; we're stating a fact about reality that we commit ourselves to living by.

[Placeholder insight] Why do we say Shema at night as well as morning? Because Hashem's unity doesn't change with the light. Morning davening is easy — the world looks ordered. Maariv is the harder declaration: Hashem is One even in the dark.

segments.heText: אֲדֹנָי שְׂפָתַי תִּפְתָּח, וּפִי יַגִּיד תְּהִלָּתֶךָ.

segments.enText: [Placeholder] The evening Amidah is said quietly, each person at their own pace. There is no chazaras hashatz (repetition) at night — Maariv was originally optional, and the individual nature of the evening prayer still echoes that history.
